title_en: Sharh Shudur adh-Dhahab: Ibn Hisham's Syntax Text
title_ar: شرح شذور الذهب: متن ابن هشام في النحو
title_id: Syarh Syudur adh-Dhahab: Teks Sintaksis Ibn Hisyam
content_id:
Syarh Syudur adh-Dhahab fi Ma'rifat Kalam al-Arab ('Syarah Nugget-Nugget Emas untuk Memahami Perkataan Orang Arab') mewakili salah satu kontribusi terpenting Ibn Hisyam al-Ansari bagi pendidikan tata bahasa Arab. Karya ini terdiri dari dua bagian: teks dasar (Syudur adh-Dhahab itu sendiri, juga disebut Matn) yang disusun sebagai primer tata bahasa mandiri, dan sebuah syarah (Syarh) yang Ibn Hisyam tulis atas teksnya sendiri untuk memperluas dan menjelaskannya. Praktik menulis syarah-mandiri adalah ciri khas metode pedagogis Ibn Hisyam — ia ingin menyediakan kepada para pelajar formulasi ringkas sekaligus penjelasan lengkap.

Ibn Hisyam (708–761 H) adalah, sebagaimana telah kami catat dalam entri tentang Qatr an-Nada, ahli tata bahasa terbesar dari periode abad pertengahan menurut penilaian para ahli sezamannya. Syudur adh-Dhahab menempati posisi berbeda dalam korpusnya dibanding Qatr an-Nada: di mana Qatr adalah tata bahasa menengah yang mencakup semua topik besar sintaksis Arab, Syudur adh-Dhahab berfokus lebih khusus pada sintaksis (al-jumlah dan jenisnya) dan pada analisis kalimat-kalimat Arab — keterampilan yang dibutuhkan pelajar untuk membaca dan mengurai teks-teks Arab klasik daripada sekadar mempelajari aturan-aturan gramatikal abstrak.

Metafora judul adalah ciri khas budaya sastra Arab klasik. 'Nugget-nugget emas' (syudur, secara harfiah 'fragmen-fragmen yang tersebar') mengacu pada sifat terkonsentrasi dan terkompresi dari aturan-aturan gramatikal yang disajikan dalam teks — seperti nugget emas yang mewakili material paling berharga dalam bentuk paling esensialnya. Sub-judul 'untuk memahami perkataan orang Arab' menandakan orientasi praktis teks: ini bukan terutama tata bahasa teoritis tetapi alat untuk mencapai pemahaman aktual atas teks-teks Arab.

Syarh adalah perluasan Ibn Hisyam atas nugget-nuggetnya sendiri: setiap aturan ringkas dalam Matn diambil dalam Syarh dan diberi perlakuan penuh yang layak, dengan contoh-contoh, analisis, dan keterlibatan dengan perdebatan-perdebatan dalam tradisi gramatikal. Perluasan ini murah hati — Syarh jauh lebih panjang dari Matn — dan mencerminkan komitmen Ibn Hisyam untuk menyediakan kepada pelajar semua yang mereka butuhkan untuk memahami aturan dan menerapkannya dengan benar.

Syudur adh-Dhahab telah digunakan di seluruh dunia Islam di lembaga-lembaga yang menempatkannya bersama Qatr an-Nada dalam kurikulum tata bahasa mereka. Beberapa guru menggunakan satu dan bukan yang lain; beberapa menggunakan keduanya. Penekanan berbeda dari kedua teks — cakupan topikal komprehensif Qatr versus fokus Syudur pada analisis kalimat — menjadikan keduanya benar-benar saling melengkapi.
content_en:
Sharh Shudur adh-Dhahab fi Ma'rifat Kalam al-Arab ('Commentary on the Gold Nuggets for Understanding the Speech of the Arabs') represents one of the most important contributions of Ibn Hisham al-Ansari to Arabic grammar education. The work exists in two parts: a base text (the Shudur adh-Dhahab itself, also called the Matn) which was composed as a self-standing grammar primer, and a commentary (the Sharh) which Ibn Hisham wrote on his own text to expand and explain it. The practice of writing a self-commentary was characteristic of Ibn Hisham's pedagogical method — he wanted to provide students both the compact formulation and the full explanation.

Ibn Hisham (708–761 AH) was, as we have noted in the entry on Qatr an-Nada, the greatest grammarian of the medieval period by the assessment of his contemporaries. The Shudur adh-Dhahab occupies a different position in his corpus than the Qatr an-Nada: where the Qatr is an intermediate grammar covering all the major topics of Arabic syntax, the Shudur adh-Dhahab focuses more specifically on syntax (al-jumlah and its types) and on the analysis of Arabic sentences — skills that students need to read and parse classical Arabic texts rather than just learn abstract grammatical rules.

The title metaphor is characteristic of classical Arabic literary culture. 'Gold nuggets' (shudur, literally 'fragments scattered about') refers to the concentrated, compressed nature of the grammatical rules presented in the text — like gold nuggets that represent the most valuable material in its most essential form. The subtitle 'for understanding the speech of the Arabs' signals the text's practical orientation: it is not primarily a theoretical grammar but a tool for achieving actual comprehension of Arabic texts.

The Sharh is Ibn Hisham's expansion of his own nuggets: each compact rule in the Matn is taken up in the Sharh and given the full treatment it deserves, with examples, analysis, and engagement with the debates in the grammatical tradition. This expansion is generous — the Sharh is considerably longer than the Matn — and reflects Ibn Hisham's commitment to providing students with everything they need to understand the rule and apply it correctly.

The Shudur adh-Dhahab has been used across the Islamic world in institutions that placed it alongside the Qatr an-Nada in their grammar curricula. Some teachers used one and not the other; some used both. The different emphases of the two texts — the Qatr's comprehensive topical coverage versus the Shudur's focus on sentence analysis — made them genuinely complementary.
content_ar: Sharh Shudur adh-Dhahab fi Ma'rifat علم الكلام al-Arab ('Commentary on the Gold Nuggets for الفهم the Speech of the Arabs') represents one of the most المهم contributions of Ibn Hisham al-Ansari to Arabic grammar education. The work exists in two parts: a base text (the Shudur adh-Dhahab itself, أيضاً called the المتن) which was composed as a self-standing grammar primer, and a commentary (the Sharh) which Ibn Hisham wrote on his own text to expand and explain it. The practice of writing a self-commentary was characteristic of Ibn Hisham's pedagogical method — he wanted to provide students both the compact formulation and the full explanation. Ibn Hisham (708–761 AH) was, as we have noted in the entry on Qatr an-Nada, the greatest grammarian of the medieval period by the assessment of his contemporaries. The Shudur adh-Dhahab occupies a different position in his corpus than the Qatr an-Nada: where the Qatr is an intermediate grammar covering all the major topics of Arabic syntax, the Shudur adh-Dhahab focuses more specifically on syntax (al-jumlah and its types) and on the analysis of Arabic sentences — skills that students need to read and parse الكلاسيكي Arabic texts rather than just learn abstract grammatical rules. The title metaphor is characteristic of الكلاسيكي Arabic literary culture. 'Gold nuggets' (shudur, literally 'fragments scattered about') refers to the concentrated, compressed nature of the grammatical rules presented in the text — like gold nuggets that represent the most valuable material in its most الجوهري form. The subtitle 'for الفهم the speech of the Arabs' signals the text's practical orientation: it is not primarily a theoretical grammar but a tool for achieving actual comprehension of Arabic texts. The Sharh is Ibn Hisham's expansion of his own nuggets: each compact rule in the المتن is taken up in the Sharh and given the full treatment it deserves, with examples, analysis, and engagement with the debates in the grammatical التراث. This expansion is generous — the Sharh is considerably longer than the المتن — and reflects Ibn Hisham's commitment to providing students with everything they need لفهم the rule and apply it correctly. The Shudur adh-Dhahab has been used across the Islamic الدنيا in institutions that placed it alongside the Qatr an-Nada in their grammar curricula.
